content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

«And why does that approach hold in this case?»
That was the question. Nothing aggressive, nothing unfair. The person who asked it wasn’t trying to catch me – they just wanted to understand. And I stood there, in a meeting I had prepared, about research I had supposedly gone through, and I could barely answer.
I want to be clear about what made this moment strange. I had prepared. I had gone through the material. I had covered everything, and faster than ever, because this time I leaned on an AI tool to do the research with me. I moved quickly, I told myself I was saving time, and I walked into that room feeling ready.
The question took maybe six seconds to expose all of it.
Because here is what had actually happened, and it took me a while to admit it: the speed I got made me think I knew a lot, when actually I knew a little. The tool gave me velocity and I mistook velocity for understanding. They are not the same thing. They were never the same thing. But when you’re moving fast, they feel identical – right up until someone asks you why.
That is where this essay comes from. Because the mistake I made in that room is, I think, the same mistake we are about to make collectively, at scale, with a smile on our face.
So let me say the idea plainly. Even if we have a tool like Claude, it won’t rewire how we think. It won’t change the person we are underneath. It will change our speed, yes. It will change our output. But the balance – the real balance – stays the same. And sometimes it hides a new gap we don’t see coming.
The developer who was always a bit behind
Take a developer. Let’s assume that before all this, he was not as fast as the others. He was struggling – not because he was lazy, not because he didn’t care, but because of the way he was thinking. Maybe he needed more time to hold the whole problem in his head. Maybe he approached things in a way that didn’t fit how the team worked. Whatever the reason, when you looked at the group, he was behind.
Now give him Claude.
He becomes faster. Of course he does. The tasks that used to take a full day now take a few hours. He ships more, he unblocks himself, and from the outside it looks like a small miracle.
But his colleagues also got Claude. The ones who were already fast became even faster. The ones who were thinking clearly are now thinking clearly and quickly. So yes, our developer improved. But he improved from a lower place, and everyone else improved from a higher place.
Zoom out, and the company looks great. The average went up, velocity is higher, the dashboards are green. Everybody is producing more.
But zoom in. Zoom in on the people, and you see the same picture as before. The fast ones are still ahead, the one who was struggling is still, relatively, struggling. The gap didn’t close. It just moved to a new floor. Same distances, same balance, only faster.
This is the part we forget when we get excited about these tools. A tool that helps everyone at once doesn’t change anyone’s position. It lifts the whole line, but the line keeps its shape.
«But maybe it closes the gap»
Here someone will push back, and it’s a fair push. Maybe the tool helps the weaker ones more. Maybe it lifts the person who was behind more than the person who was ahead, because the one who was ahead didn’t need as much help in the first place. So maybe, actually, the gap gets smaller.
Fine. Let’s say that’s true, at least sometimes. But watch what happens then, because this is where it gets interesting.
Even if the visible gap closes – the gap in output, in speed, in what shows up on the dashboard – a different gap opens up underneath, and this one is invisible. It is the gap between what you appear to know and what you actually know. The tool can make your work look like the work of someone who understands. It cannot make you the person who understands. So on the surface you catch up, and inside you fall behind, and nobody sees it, not even you.
Until the question comes.
The tool can close the gap you can see and quietly open the one you can’t.
And the one you can’t see is the one that decides everything, in the end. I know exactly how long it takes for that hidden gap to become visible: about six seconds, in a meeting room.
What we actually mean by «hero»
When we imagine the hero, we imagine the one who stands out. Not ahead by a little, ahead by a lot. The one everybody points to. And deep down, when we buy the tool, this is the fantasy we are buying. We think: now, finally, I will be that person.
But a tool that everyone has cannot make you exceptional. Say it out loud and it’s almost obvious, but we never say it out loud. If everyone holds the same weapon, the weapon is not what makes the difference anymore. It becomes the floor. It becomes the thing you need just to stay in the room – not the thing that lifts you above it.
The heroes, if we want to use that word, were never the ones with the best tools. They were the ones who understood something the others didn’t. Who saw the problem from an angle nobody else was standing at. And you don’t get that angle by going faster. You get it by understanding, and understanding is exactly the thing the tool cannot hand you.
So no, you won’t become a hero using Claude. Nobody will.
Then what is it actually good for?
I don’t want this to sound like I’m against these tools. I use Claude, it helps me, and I’m writing about the wrong way to use it precisely because I fell into it myself. So let me be precise about the right way.
The best thing you can do with a tool like this is not to go faster. It is to learn.
There is a real difference between asking Claude to give you the answer, and asking Claude to make you understand it. In the first case you save twenty minutes and you learn nothing. In the second you maybe spend the same twenty minutes, but you come out understanding something you didn’t understand before, and that stays with you. It’s yours now. Next time you might not even need the tool.
This is the only kind of use that changes the balance. Because it doesn’t make you faster at the person you already are – it slowly turns you into a different person, one who understands more. And that is the one thing a tool available to everyone still can’t copy from one head to another: what you actually took the time to learn.
If there is a way to get better – not faster, better – it goes through learning. It always did. Nothing about AI changed that. If anything, AI made it easier to avoid learning, which is a strange trap: the tool that could teach you the most is also the one that tempts you the most to skip the lesson.
The one thing you can’t buy back
And this is where it gets hard, because learning has a cost, and the cost is time.
You can’t learn without time. Maybe an AI shortens the path a little. Maybe it explains in five minutes what a book would explain in an hour. Fine. But you still have to sit with it, still have to try, fail, be confused, come back. There is no understanding that arrives instantly. The time can be reduced. It cannot be removed.
Now look at how we actually use these tools. We use them to gain time – that’s the whole pitch, do it faster, do more, save hours. And then we take the time we saved and we spend it, on more tasks, more features, more messages. We treat the saved time like money we’re allowed to burn.
But here is the paradox. The time we save is exactly the time we would need in order to learn. If we always spend it on going faster, we never invest it in getting better. And if we never get better, we stay exactly where we were – only more tired, and more dependent on the tool.
That was my mistake with the meeting. I used the tool to save time, I spent the saved time on nothing that mattered, and I never paid the small cost that would have let me answer the question. The whole thing could have been avoided. Not by using the tool less. By using it to understand more, instead of to finish sooner.
So if we use AI only to always gain time, in the end we lose everything. We lose the very thing the time was supposed to buy us. We optimize ourselves into standing still.
Where this leaves us
I keep coming back to that developer. He’s faster now. On paper everything improved. But if he pours every hour the tool gives back into doing more of the same, in a year he’ll be the same developer – just quicker at being who he already was.
But if he takes some of that time and puts it back into learning, the slow way, the way that costs something, then something else can happen. Not because the tool made him a hero. The tool never does that. Because he used it to buy the one resource that matters, and then spent it on the right thing.
I should be honest about what I haven’t resolved here. Sometimes speed is exactly what you need. The boilerplate nobody learns anything from, the task you’ve done a hundred times, the deadline that is real – going fast there is not a sin, and I won’t pretend I know where the line sits between the time you’re allowed to save and the time you’re stealing from yourself. I don’t think anyone knows yet. Maybe that line is different for each of us, and the only way to find it is to get a question you can’t answer, once, in a room full of people.
What I do know is this: the tool gives us time, and what we do with that time is what we become.
You won’t become a hero using Claude. The tool lifts everyone at once, and lifting everyone at once changes nothing about who stands where – and worse, it can make you feel like you know a lot while you quietly know a little. But you can use it to understand, in the time it hands back to you. That won’t make you a hero either.
It will just make you harder to catch off guard. And if you’ve ever stood in a room, six seconds into a silence you can’t fill, you already know that’s worth more than any hour you thought you were saving.
